text: Which device role describes a machine that requests resources from another machine on the network?
explanation: A client requests resources; a server provides them. The roles describe behavior — the same machine can act as both.

text: Which network type connects devices within a single building and is owned by the organization using it?
explanation: A Local Area Network covers a single site (home, office, building) and is owned/operated by the organization itself. WANs span cities or more and typically involve provider circuits.

text: A company connects its two city offices using a leased circuit from a service provider. Each office has its own switched network. What describes the leased circuit?
explanation: Connectivity between geographically separate sites over a provider's infrastructure is a WAN link. The office networks at each end are LANs.

text: Which device forwards frames within a LAN based on MAC addresses?
explanation: Switches operate at Layer 2, using MAC addresses to forward frames between devices inside the same network.

text: Which device connects different networks and selects paths using IP addresses?
explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3, maintaining routing tables and forwarding packets between networks based on IP addresses.

text: A company needs to allow wireless laptops onto the wired LAN and enforce security policy on traffic entering from the Internet. Which two devices most directly provide these functions?
explanation: An access point bridges wireless clients onto the wired LAN; a firewall enforces security policy on traffic crossing the network boundary.

text: In which topology does every end device connect to one central device?
explanation: A star topology connects all devices to a central point (typically a switch) — the standard modern LAN design.

text: How many links are required to create a full mesh between 8 routers?
explanation: Full mesh needs n(n−1)/2 links: 8×7/2 = 28. This quadratic growth is why full mesh is reserved for small cores.

text: A cable cut disconnects exactly one workstation while all others continue working. Which topology is in use?
explanation: In a star, each device has a dedicated cable to the central switch — one cut affects one device. In a bus, one break kills the whole segment.

text: In the three-tier campus design, at which layer do end devices connect and PoE phones receive power?
explanation: The access layer is where end devices plug in — lots of ports, PoE, and per-port security. Distribution aggregates; core switches fast.

text: Which architecture guarantees any server is exactly two hops from any other and is optimized for east-west traffic?
explanation: In spine-leaf, every leaf connects to every spine — any two servers communicate leaf→spine→leaf with consistent latency. It's the data-center standard.

text: A two-tier campus design merges which two layers?
explanation: The collapsed-core (two-tier) design merges core and distribution into one layer — appropriate for mid-size networks that don't need a separate backbone.

text: What is the maximum segment length for UTP Ethernet cabling?
explanation: All copper Ethernet standards specify a 100 m maximum segment. Longer runs need fiber or an intermediate device.

text: Which cable classically connects a switch to another switch?
explanation: Like devices (switch-switch, PC-PC) classically require a crossover cable, swapping the transmit and receive pairs. Auto-MDIX now compensates automatically, but the exam tests the classic rules.

text: You must connect two buildings 300 m apart inside a campus. Which medium is the most appropriate minimum?
explanation: 300 m exceeds copper's 100 m limit but is within multimode fiber's range (~550 m at 1G). Single-mode also works but costs more — multimode is the appropriate choice.

text: Users on a new link report it is up but extremely slow with late collisions and CRC errors. What is the classic cause?
options: Duplex mismatch between the two ends
explanation: A duplex mismatch (one side full, one half) produces a working but terrible link: the half-duplex side detects 'collisions' and errors accumulate. Check with 'show interfaces'.

text: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for logical addressing and path determination?
explanation: Layer 3 (Network) handles IP addressing and routing — choosing paths between networks.

text: At which OSI layer does a switch make its forwarding decisions?
explanation: Switches read destination MAC addresses in frame headers — Layer 2, the Data Link layer.

text: TCP port numbers belong to which OSI layer?
explanation: Port numbers address applications end-to-end and belong to Layer 4, the Transport layer, alongside TCP and UDP.

text: What is the PDU at the Network layer called?
explanation: PDUs by layer: L4 segment, L3 packet, L2 frame, L1 bits. The Network layer PDU is the packet.

text: A packet crosses two routers between source and destination hosts. How many times is a new Layer 2 frame built in total?
explanation: Each link needs its own frame: host→R1, R1→R2, R2→host = 3 frames. The IP packet inside stays intact end-to-end; the L2 envelope is rebuilt per hop.

text: Which addresses change as a packet passes through a router (no NAT involved)?
explanation: Routers rebuild the frame for each new link, so both MAC addresses change per hop. The IP addresses remain the same end-to-end unless NAT rewrites them.

text: Which TCP/IP model layer combines the functions of the OSI Application, Presentation, and Session layers?
explanation: The TCP/IP Application layer absorbs OSI layers 5–7. Network Access absorbs OSI 1–2.

text: A PC sends traffic to a server on a remote network. Which destination MAC address does it use in the frame?
explanation: MAC addressing is local-link only. For remote destinations the frame is addressed to the default gateway's MAC while the destination IP remains the server's.

text: What is the purpose of the FCS field in an Ethernet frame?
explanation: The Frame Check Sequence is a CRC in the trailer. Receivers recompute it; a mismatch means corruption and the frame is silently discarded.

text: What is the default maximum payload (MTU) of a standard Ethernet frame?
explanation: The default MTU is 1500 bytes of payload. (1518 is the full classic frame including headers/FCS; 9000 refers to jumbo frames.)

text: Which destination MAC address indicates a broadcast frame?
explanation: All-ones (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) is the broadcast address — every host on the LAN processes it. 01:00:5E... prefixes indicate multicast.

text: What does ARP resolve?
explanation: The Address Resolution Protocol finds the MAC address that owns a known IP address on the local network. (DNS handles names.)

text: How is an ARP request delivered, and how is the reply delivered?
explanation: The request is broadcast (the sender doesn't know the target's MAC yet); the owner replies directly via unicast, and both sides cache the mapping.

text: What does a switch record in its MAC address table when a frame arrives?
options: The destination MAC and arrival port
The source MAC and arrival port
explanation: Switches learn from SOURCE MACs — mapping the sender's MAC to the port the frame arrived on. Destinations are looked up, not learned.

text: A switch receives a unicast frame whose destination MAC is not in its table. What does it do?
options: Floods it out all ports except the arrival port
Forwards it to the default gateway
explanation: Unknown unicast frames are flooded everywhere except the source port. The eventual reply teaches the switch the destination's port.

text: A 24-port switch has 12 ports in VLAN 10 and 12 in VLAN 20, all connected to PCs. How many collision domains and broadcast domains exist?
explanation: Every switch port is its own collision domain (24). Each VLAN is a separate broadcast domain (2).

text: What does each VLAN on a switch correspond to?
explanation: A VLAN is a broadcast domain — broadcasts stay inside it, and crossing VLANs requires a Layer 3 device.

text: Two PCs on the same switch but different VLANs have correct IP settings yet cannot communicate. What is required?
options: A Layer 3 device to route between VLANs
explanation: VLANs isolate at Layer 2 by design. Inter-VLAN traffic must be routed — by a router or Layer 3 switch.

text: What does the 802.1Q standard define?
explanation: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte VLAN tag into frames crossing trunk links so the receiving switch knows each frame's VLAN. (802.1X is authentication; 802.3ad is aggregation; 802.1D is STP.)

text: Which VLAN's frames cross an 802.1Q trunk without a tag?
options: No VLAN — all frames are tagged
explanation: The native VLAN (default VLAN 1, configurable) traverses trunks untagged. Both ends must agree on it, or frames leak between VLANs.

text: Why do security best practices recommend disabling DTP on switch ports?
options: An attacker could negotiate a trunk and access all VLANs
explanation: Dynamic Trunking Protocol lets a connected device negotiate trunking. An attacker exploiting this sees every VLAN's traffic. Hard-code access/trunk modes and use 'switchport nonegotiate'.

text: In a router-on-a-stick design, what connects the router to the switch?
options: A single trunk link carrying all VLANs
explanation: ROAS uses one physical interface split into dot1q subinterfaces, connected via a single 802.1Q trunk to the switch.

text: Which feature lets a Layer 3 switch act as the default gateway for a VLAN?
explanation: A Switched Virtual Interface (SVI) is a virtual L3 interface per VLAN on a multilayer switch — assigned the gateway IP for that VLAN.

text: What problem does Spanning Tree Protocol prevent?
explanation: Redundant L2 links create loops; since frames have no TTL, broadcasts multiply into a storm. STP blocks redundant ports to break loops.

text: How is the STP root bridge elected?
options: Lowest bridge ID (priority, then lowest MAC)
explanation: Lowest bridge ID wins: priority is compared first (default 32768), with the lowest MAC breaking ties.

text: A BPDU arrives on a PortFast port with BPDU Guard enabled. What happens?
explanation: BPDU Guard assumes host ports should never receive BPDUs — one arriving means a switch was plugged in. The port goes err-disabled immediately.

text: Which STP improvement converges in about 1–2 seconds and runs per-VLAN on Cisco switches by default?
explanation: Rapid PVST+ implements RSTP (802.1w) per VLAN — Cisco's default — replacing classic STP's 30–50 s convergence with 1–2 s.

text: SW1 uses LACP mode 'active'; SW2 uses 'passive'. Does the EtherChannel form?
explanation: LACP forms when at least one side initiates: active+active or active+passive work. Passive+passive fails because nobody starts the negotiation.

text: What does STP see when four physical links are bundled into a port-channel?
explanation: The bundle appears as a single logical interface to STP, so no member is blocked — all forward simultaneously with load balancing.

text: How many bits are in an IPv4 address?
explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits, written as four 8-bit octets in dotted decimal (0–255 each).

text: Which of these is a private (RFC 1918) address?
explanation: Private ranges: 10/8, 172.16–172.31 (/12), 192.168/16. 172.20.x.x falls inside 172.16.0.0/12; 172.32 and 192.169 are just outside their lookalike ranges.

text: A workstation shows the address 169.254.77.10. What does this indicate?
options: It failed to reach a DHCP server (APIPA)
explanation: 169.254.0.0/16 is the self-assigned APIPA range — the OS falls back to it when no DHCP reply arrives. Investigate DHCP reachability.

text: How many usable host addresses does a /26 subnet provide?
explanation: /26 leaves 6 host bits: 2⁶ = 64 addresses, minus network and broadcast = 62 usable.

text: What is the network address for host 192.168.1.77/27?
explanation: /27 → block size 32 (256−224). Multiples: 0, 32, 64, 96. 77 falls in the 64 block → network 192.168.1.64, broadcast .95.

text: You need subnets supporting at least 100 hosts each from 172.16.0.0/16. Which mask gives the most subnets while meeting the requirement?
explanation: 100 hosts requires 7 host bits (2⁷−2 = 126; 6 bits gives only 62). 32−7 = /25, yielding 512 subnets of 126 hosts.

text: What is the broadcast address of the subnet containing 10.1.37.14/19?
explanation: /19 → block size 32 in the third octet. 37 falls in the 32 block → network 10.1.32.0/19, next block starts at 64.0, so broadcast is 10.1.63.255.

text: Which mask is written as /30 in CIDR notation?
explanation: /30 = 30 ones = 255.255.255.252, leaving 2 host bits (2 usable hosts) — the classic point-to-point link mask.

text: When allocating subnets with VLSM, why should you assign the largest subnets first?
options: Big blocks must start on their own alignment boundaries; placing them first avoids overlaps
explanation: A /25 can only start at .0 or .128. If smaller subnets are scattered first, no valid boundary may remain. Largest-first guarantees clean alignment.
Four consecutive /24s starting at a multiple of 4 (4–7) share their first 22 bits → 10.1.4.0/22.

text: How many bits are in an IPv6 address?
explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits — eight groups of four hex digits.

text: Which rule about the :: notation in IPv6 is correct?
options: It replaces exactly one run of consecutive zero groups and may appear only once
It's only valid at the end of an address
explanation: Double-colon compresses one contiguous run of all-zero groups. Using it twice would make the address ambiguous.

text: An interface automatically configures the address fe80::5054:ff:fe12:3456. What type of address is this?
explanation: fe80::/10 is the link-local range — automatically present on every IPv6 interface and never routed off its link.

text: Which mechanism lets an IPv6 host build its own global address from a router advertisement, without any DHCP server?
explanation: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration: the host takes the /64 prefix from the RA and appends its own interface ID (EUI-64 or random).

text: What replaces ARP in IPv6?
explanation: The Neighbor Discovery Protocol (over ICMPv6) uses Neighbor Solicitation and Advertisement messages via multicast — IPv6 has no broadcast at all.

text: A router has routes 10.0.0.0/8, 10.1.0.0/16, and 10.1.1.0/24. Which does it use for a packet to 10.1.1.55?
explanation: Longest prefix match: the most specific route (/24) wins regardless of AD or metric.

text: OSPF and EIGRP both offer a route to the same prefix. Which one is installed, and why?
explanation: For the SAME prefix, administrative distance decides: EIGRP's 90 beats OSPF's 110.

text: What does the routing table code 'C' indicate?
explanation: 'C' marks connected networks — created automatically when an interface with an IP comes up. They have AD 0.

text: Which command creates a default route toward 203.0.113.1?
explanation: 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>' matches every destination not covered by a more specific route.

text: What is the purpose of configuring 'ip route 10.5.0.0 255.255.0.0 192.0.2.9 200' when OSPF already advertises 10.5.0.0/16?
options: A floating static backup that activates only if the OSPF route disappears
It is invalid — AD can't exceed 110
explanation: With AD 200 (worse than OSPF's 110), the static stays dormant until the OSPF route vanishes — a designed backup path.

text: Which parameters must MATCH for two routers to become OSPF neighbors?
options: Hello/dead timers, area ID, and subnet
explanation: Neighbors require matching area, subnet, timers, and authentication. Router IDs must be UNIQUE, and process IDs are locally significant.

text: How does OSPF calculate its metric?
options: Sum of interface costs based on bandwidth
explanation: OSPF cost = reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth, summed across the path. Faster links = lower cost.

text: Routers on a LAN: R1 (priority 0), R2 (priority 1, RID 9.9.9.9), R3 (priority 1, RID 2.2.2.2). Who is elected DR?
explanation: Priority 0 = never DR (R1 out). Between R2 and R3 (equal priority), highest router ID wins: 9.9.9.9.

text: Which command enables OSPF directly on an interface (modern style)?
explanation: 'ip ospf <process> area <area>' in interface config mode activates OSPF without wildcard-mask network statements.

text: All OSPF areas must connect to which area?
explanation: OSPF's hierarchy requires every area to touch the backbone, Area 0. ABRs connect other areas to it.

text: In HSRP, what do hosts use as their default gateway?
options: A virtual IP shared by the router group
explanation: Hosts point at the virtual IP (with its virtual MAC). Whichever router is Active answers for it — failover is invisible to hosts.

text: R1 (priority 110) fails and R2 becomes HSRP Active. R1 recovers but R2 stays Active. Why?
options: Preemption is not configured on R1
explanation: HSRP preemption is OFF by default — a recovered router won't reclaim Active without 'standby preempt', regardless of priority.

text: Which FHRP is an open standard?
explanation: VRRP is the IETF standard. HSRP and GLBP are Cisco-proprietary (GLBP adds per-host load balancing).

text: Which protocol provides reliable, ordered delivery with acknowledgments?
explanation: TCP numbers every byte, acknowledges receipt, retransmits losses, and delivers data in order. UDP is best-effort.

text: What is the correct order of the TCP three-way handshake?
explanation: Client sends SYN, server answers SYN-ACK, client completes with ACK — connection established.

text: Why do voice and video applications prefer UDP?
options: Retransmitted real-time data arrives too late to be useful; UDP avoids that delay
explanation: Real-time media is latency-sensitive: TCP's retransmission and ordering add delay for data whose moment has passed. UDP drops and moves on.

text: Which port does HTTPS use by default?
explanation: HTTPS = HTTP over TLS on TCP 443. Plain HTTP uses TCP 80.

text: Which port and protocol does SSH use?
explanation: SSH runs on TCP 22. (Telnet is TCP 23 — unencrypted; SMTP is TCP 25.)

text: DHCP uses which pair of UDP ports?
explanation: DHCP servers listen on UDP 67; clients use UDP 68. (53 = DNS, 161/162 = SNMP, 20/21 = FTP.)

text: Which DNS record maps a hostname to an IPv4 address?
explanation: A records map names to IPv4; AAAA to IPv6; MX identifies mail servers; PTR does reverse (IP→name) lookups.

text: Users can ping servers by IP but not by name. Which service is the prime suspect?
explanation: Working IP connectivity with failing name-based access is the signature of a DNS problem — wrong or unreachable resolver, or missing records.

text: What is the correct order of DHCP lease messages?
explanation: DORA: the client Discovers, servers Offer, the client Requests one offer, the server Acknowledges.

text: Clients in VLAN 40 can't get leases from the central DHCP server at 10.50.0.10. Local VLAN clients work fine. What's the fix?
options: Configure 'ip helper-address 10.50.0.10' on the VLAN 40 gateway interface
Add a default route to the clients
explanation: DHCP Discovers are broadcasts and don't cross routers. The relay agent (ip helper-address) on the client-facing interface unicasts them to the server.

text: In Cisco NAT terminology, what is the 'inside global' address?
options: The private address of an inside host
The public address representing an inside host after translation
The address of the outside server
explanation: Inside = owned by your network; global = as seen from outside. Inside global is your host's public, post-translation identity.

text: Which NAT variant lets hundreds of hosts share one public IP simultaneously?
explanation: Port Address Translation multiplexes many private hosts onto one public IP by rewriting source ports — the 'overload' keyword in IOS.

text: A PAT config translates traffic, but only from some hosts. Which component most likely excludes the others?
options: The ACL that selects source addresses for translation
explanation: The ACL referenced by 'ip nat inside source list' defines WHICH sources get translated — hosts outside its permit ranges are simply not NATted.

text: You configure 'logging trap 3' on a router. Which messages reach the syslog server?
explanation: The trap level is a severity ceiling counting toward zero: levels 0 (emergency) through 3 (error) are sent; less-severe messages are filtered.

text: What does NTP synchronize across network devices?
explanation: The Network Time Protocol (UDP 123) synchronizes device clocks — essential for correlating logs and certificates.

text: Which SNMP version adds authentication and encryption?
explanation: SNMPv3 introduces real security (auth + privacy). v1/v2c use cleartext community strings.

text: What is jitter?
explanation: Jitter is delay VARIATION — packets arriving at irregular intervals. Voice needs jitter under ~30 ms to sound smooth.

text: What is the key difference between policing and shaping?
options: Policing buffers excess; shaping drops it
Policing drops or re-marks excess; shaping buffers and delays it
explanation: Police = enforce a ceiling by dropping/re-marking. Shape = smooth traffic to a rate by queueing the excess. Police drops, shape delays.

text: Which DSCP marking and queuing treatment is standard for voice traffic?
explanation: Voice gets Expedited Forwarding (DSCP 46) and a low-latency priority queue so it always transmits first.

text: Which element of the CIA triad does a DoS attack target?
explanation: Denial of Service exhausts resources so legitimate users can't use the system — an attack on availability.

text: An attacker sends a tailored fraudulent email specifically to the CFO. What is this called?
explanation: Spear phishing aimed at executives is whaling. Generic mass email = phishing; phone-based = vishing; physical door-following = tailgating.

text: Which attack fills a server's connection table with half-open connections?
explanation: A SYN flood sends endless SYNs without completing handshakes, exhausting the server's half-open connection resources.

text: Which AAA protocol encrypts its entire payload and separates authentication, authorization, and accounting?
explanation: TACACS+ (TCP 49) encrypts the full payload and separates all three A's — ideal for device administration. RADIUS encrypts only the password.

text: Which steps are prerequisites for enabling SSH on a Cisco device?
options: Configure hostname, domain name, and generate RSA keys
explanation: The RSA keypair (crypto key generate rsa) requires a hostname and ip domain name to be set first. Then restrict VTYs with 'transport input ssh'.

text: How does a router process an ACL when a packet matches multiple lines?
options: The FIRST matching line wins and processing stops
explanation: ACLs evaluate top-down; the first match decides the action and evaluation stops. Line order is everything.

text: A packet matches no line in an applied ACL. What happens?
options: It is dropped by the implicit deny
explanation: Every ACL ends with an invisible 'deny any'. Unmatched traffic is silently discarded — remember to permit what you want to keep.

text: Where should a STANDARD ACL be placed and why?
options: Close to the source — it's precise
Close to the destination — it matches only source addresses and would over-block near the source
explanation: Standard ACLs filter on source only. Applied near the source they'd block traffic to ALL destinations, so they belong near the destination. Extended ACLs go near the source.

text: Which port-security violation mode drops offending frames and increments a counter WITHOUT disabling the port?
explanation: Restrict drops + logs + counts. Protect drops silently. Shutdown (default) err-disables the whole port.

text: After enabling DHCP snooping, legitimate clients stop receiving leases. What was most likely forgotten?
options: Marking the uplink to the real DHCP server as trusted
explanation: All ports default to untrusted, which blocks server-type DHCP messages — including the real server's Offers arriving via the uplink. Trust the uplink port.

text: Which feature validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table to stop spoofing?
explanation: DAI checks ARP claims against known IP↔MAC bindings on untrusted ports, defeating ARP-spoofing MITM attacks.

text: Which technology is required to run OSPF between two sites over an encrypted Internet tunnel?
explanation: OSPF uses multicast, which classic IPsec can't carry. GRE encapsulates any traffic (multicast included) and IPsec encrypts the GRE tunnel.

text: Which VPN type typically serves traveling employees connecting from laptops?
explanation: Remote-access VPNs (usually TLS-based, e.g. Cisco Secure Client) connect individual users; site-to-site IPsec connects whole networks.

text: Which 2.4 GHz channels do not overlap?
explanation: 2.4 GHz channels are 5 MHz apart but ~20 MHz wide, so only 1, 6, and 11 coexist without interference.

text: What protocol tunnels traffic between a lightweight AP and its wireless LAN controller?
explanation: CAPWAP (UDP 5246 control / 5247 data) carries both management and, typically, client data between AP and WLC.

text: Which wireless security standard introduced SAE to defeat offline dictionary attacks?
explanation: WPA3 replaced the PSK 4-way handshake with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, adding forward secrecy and resistance to offline guessing.

text: A company wants per-user wireless authentication with individually revocable credentials. What should it deploy?
explanation: Enterprise mode authenticates each user against RADIUS via 802.1X/EAP — disable one account, lock out one person. PSK shares one secret with everyone.

text: Which hypervisor type runs directly on server hardware?
explanation: Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisors like ESXi and KVM run on the hardware itself; Type 2 (VirtualBox) runs atop a host OS.

text: What is the key difference between containers and virtual machines?
options: Containers share the host OS kernel instead of carrying a full guest OS
explanation: Containers virtualize at the OS level — sharing the kernel makes them megabytes in size with millisecond startup, versus full guest-OS VMs.

text: A team rents virtual machines and networks from AWS but manages the operating systems themselves. Which service model is this?
explanation: Infrastructure as a Service provides compute/network/storage building blocks; the customer manages the OS and everything above it.

text: Which WAN approach steers each application over the best-performing of several transports using centralized policy?
explanation: SD-WAN continuously measures path health (loss/latency/jitter) and routes per-application across MPLS, broadband, and LTE per central policy.

text: In SDN terminology, which plane does a routing protocol like OSPF belong to?
explanation: The control plane is the 'thinking' — building tables via protocols like OSPF and STP. The data plane forwards packets using those tables.

text: A Python script calls a controller's REST API, and the controller then configures switches. Which APIs are used, in order?
explanation: Direction names are relative to the controller: apps talk to it via NORTHBOUND APIs (REST); it programs devices via SOUTHBOUND protocols (NETCONF, RESTCONF, OpenFlow).

text: Which HTTP verb retrieves data without modifying it?
explanation: GET reads a resource. POST creates, PUT/PATCH update, DELETE removes — the CRUD mapping.

text: Which of these is INVALID JSON?
explanation: JSON requires double quotes for keys and strings — single quotes are invalid. Arrays, null, nesting, and booleans are all fine.

text: A REST API returns status 401. What does it mean?
explanation: 401 Unauthorized = the request lacks valid credentials. 404 = not found, 2xx = success, 5xx = server-side errors.

text: Which configuration management tool is agentless and uses YAML playbooks pushed over SSH?
explanation: Ansible needs no agent on managed devices — it connects via SSH/API and applies YAML playbooks. Puppet/Chef use agents; Terraform focuses on declarative provisioning.

text: Running the same Ansible playbook twice produces no changes the second time. What property is this?
explanation: Idempotent operations enforce a desired state — reapplying them when the state already matches changes nothing.

text: A platform analyzes months of telemetry and predicts an uplink will saturate in two weeks. Which AI category is this?
explanation: Forecasting future state from learned historical patterns is predictive AI. Generative AI creates new content (configs, summaries) instead.

text: An assistant drafts an ACL configuration from a plain-English request. Which AI category is this, and what must follow?
options: Generative AI; review and test before deployment
explanation: Producing new configuration from intent is generative AI — and generated configs must be reviewed and tested, since models can produce plausible-looking errors.
